$comment: plan-24 Stage 7 (the cap-lift payoff) end-to-end witness. `main.c` is `return ((( ... 42 ... )));` with 300 EXPRESSION parens — a nesting depth that EXCEEDS the OLD `maxExpressionDepth` cap (256), so under the pre-Stage-7 configuration the parser tripped a positioned `P_ExpressionTooDeep` at the 256th paren and this program FAILED TO COMPILE. After Stage 7 the cap is config-driven (c-subset `.lang.json` `parser.maxExpressionDepth` = 1024, read into `ParserConfig` by `parserConfigFor` in src/analysis/compilation_unit/compilation_unit.cpp), so 300 levels is now a LEGAL parse and this compiles + runs. The shape is deliberately FLAT-FOLDING and small-frame so it is safe on EVERY target (x86_64 AND arm64, native AND emulated): each `(` is a transparent paren wrapper that the CST->HIR lowerer strips, so the whole expression folds to the single constant 42 — the emitted function is `return 42;` (a ~1.5 KB PE / ~9 KB ELF, code a few bytes), with NO deep SSA value web and NO large stack frame (the deep_stmt_mir lesson: a deep nest must lower flat, never a per-level frame, or it spills the register allocator / overflows a target stack). 300 was chosen to clear the old 256 cap with margin while staying far below the parser's still-recursive paren-arm crash floor on the 64 MiB worker (measured ~3000 on the tightest build, MSVC Debug) AND below the shipped 1024 cap, so the parse is clean on every supported build/host. The exit code 42 is the folded constant (42 mod 256 = 42); a regression that DROPPED the cap-lift would emit `P_ExpressionTooDeep` and fail this example's strict zero-diagnostic compile, and a regression that broke the paren fold would change the exit code. The `optimizedPipelines` arm proves the lowered MIR survives the full release pipeline (ConstFold collapses the constant); the baseline arm is the real-codegen RUN witness. arm64 runs under qemu on the linux leg; macho on the macos-latest leg.
